context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this pset, and honestly the whole thing feels kind of jumbled to me. Could we start super broad with Problem 1 or 2—I don’t really see how I’m supposed to know what kind of proof idea to use.
exchanges.tutor: Let's pick one and dig in. Which sounds more approachable to you right now — Problem 1 (double counting) or Problem 2 (pigeonhole)? Once you choose, tell me in your own words what the problem is actually asking you to show.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student feels overwhelmed and wants broad guidance. I should help them pick one problem to focus on and activate their prior knowledge through a Socratic question rather than explaining proof strategies for them. I'll ask them to pick one problem and then probe what they already know about it, keeping my response short and directing them to engage actively.

exchanges.student: Maybe Problem 2, but I’m not really sure because they all kind of blur together. It feels like it’s asking me to prove some pair exists for kind of a weird number-theory reason, and I don’t know how I’m supposed to invent the pigeons/holes part from that.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice. The hint says to factor each number into an odd number times a power of 2. Can you try that for a few specific numbers — say, 6, 12, and 3 — and tell me what you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen Problem 2 and given a vague but not entirely wrong intuition. Rather than explaining the pigeonhole principle or the hint to them, I should ask them to engage with the hint directly, which is the key insight for this problem. I'll push them to think about what the hint (factoring into odd * power of 2) actually means for a specific example, which is guided discovery without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I get 6 = 3·2, 12 = 3·4, and 3 = 3·1, so they all have the same odd part, I guess. That seems suggestive, but I still mostly want the big picture of why this automatically gives the whole proof instead of just these examples.
exchanges.tutor: Great observation! Now look at 6 and 12 specifically. If you divide one by the other, what do you get? And what does that tell you about why sharing the same odd part is relevant to what the problem asks you to prove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a key observation — that 6, 12, and 3 share the same odd part — and is already sensing the connection. Rather than giving them the big picture directly, I should ask them to connect this observation to the problem's goal: finding a and b where a/b is a power of 2. This keeps them doing the reasoning themselves.

exchanges.student: 12/6 = 2, so that’s a power of 2. I sort of see the pattern, but I’m still not seeing the full argument cleanly—could you just explain the whole pigeonhole setup all at once, including exactly what the pigeons and holes are?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give the full setup — that's yours to work out! But you're close. You noticed that 6, 12, and 3 all share the same odd part (3). So here's a nudge: if two numbers share the same odd part, what can you say about their ratio? And given that, what would make a natural 'hole' for each number in {1, 2, ..., 2n}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give the full answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they're very close — they've seen the key insight. I should redirect them to build the argument themselves by asking about the structure they've already uncovered, specifically what property of a number determines which 'hole' it belongs to.

exchanges.student: If two numbers have the same odd part, their ratio is \(2^a/2^b = 2^{a-b}\), so it’s a power of 2. So I guess the hole should be the odd part. But then I get stuck again because I want the whole argument to just snap into place, and I’m not sure how to state the pigeons/holes/rule without overthinking it.
exchanges.tutor: You basically have it! Stop waiting for it to snap — just try stating it explicitly: what are the pigeons, what are the holes, and what is the rule for assigning pigeons to holes? Write it out, even roughly, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all three key components implicitly: pigeons are the numbers in S, holes are the odd parts, and the rule is assigning each number to its odd part. They just need to articulate this clearly themselves. I should ask them to state it explicitly rather than doing it for them. I'll also gently push back on the 'snap into place' mentality — they need to write it out.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough try: the pigeons are the \(n+1\) elements of \(S\), and each one goes to the hole equal to its odd part after factoring out powers of 2. But then I panic about how many holes there are and whether it’s exactly \(n\) or less or something, and I kind of want a more general explanation of how people see this stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the pigeons and the rule. Now just focus on this: how many distinct odd numbers are there in {1, 2, ..., 2n}? Can you count them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making great progress and has correctly identified pigeons and the assignment rule. They're stuck on counting the holes. I should ask them a targeted question about how many distinct odd numbers exist in {1,...,2n}, which is the key counting step. I'll resist giving a general explanation of pigeonhole intuition as that's a detour from solving this specific problem.

exchanges.student: I think it’s \(n\), since the odd numbers are \(1,3,5,\dots,2n-1\). That makes it feel like the proof is basically done, but I’m weirdly still not confident about how to write the last sentence or two.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — n holes, n+1 pigeons. So what does the Pigeonhole Principle tell you must happen? And once you know that, how does it connect back to what the problem asked you to prove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has all the pieces: n+1 pigeons, n holes, correct assignment rule. They just need to connect it with the pigeonhole principle conclusion and then link back to the problem's goal (a/b is a power of 2). Rather than writing it for them, I should ask them to state the conclusion themselves.

exchanges.student: It says two elements of \(S\) go to the same odd-part hole, so they have the same odd part, and then their ratio is a power of 2. I think that’s the proof, but could you maybe give me a polished version of the whole argument in one go so I can see what a finished writeup sounds like?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it for you — that's exactly what your writeup is for! But you've got the full argument: you know the pigeons, the holes, the rule, the count, and the conclusion. Now just write your own draft from start to finish in your own words. That's the assignment. Give it a shot and you can share it if you want feedback on the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write out a polished solution for them, which I must firmly refuse — this violates the core rules. However, they've genuinely constructed the full argument. I should affirm they have the pieces and redirect them to write their own draft.

exchanges.student: Okay, that didn’t work; trying a different angle. I can draft it, but I’m also hazy on Problem 1 and I think I need the broad intuition there too—double counting always feels like magic to me, and I don’t know how people decide what set is being counted.
exchanges.tutor:
Before we move on — have you written your Problem 2 draft yet? You had the full argument. Take a few minutes to write it out first.

That said, for Problem 1: double counting always starts with identifying *one* thing to count in two different ways. Look at the right-hand side: C(a+b+c, n). What does that count, in plain English?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is shifting to Problem 1 before finishing their writeup for Problem 2. I should acknowledge the transition but first encourage them to complete Problem 2's draft, since they have all the pieces. If they genuinely want to move to Problem 1, I can do that, but I should briefly check whether they've written their Problem 2 draft yet. I'll also address their 'magic' feeling about double counting by asking a targeted question rather than giving a broad explanation.

exchanges.student: In plain English it’s choosing \(n\) things from a total of \(a+b+c\) things. But then I immediately lose the thread on the left side because the sum over \(i,j,k\) makes it feel way more complicated, and I kind of just want the full story of what object is being counted.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now imagine those a+b+c items aren't all in one pile — instead they're split into three separate groups of sizes a, b, and c. If you want to choose n items total across those three groups, how many ways can that happen? What choices do you need to make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what the RHS counts. Now I need to guide them to see the LHS as counting the same thing in a different way, by breaking the a+b+c items into three groups. I should ask a targeted question about structure rather than giving the full story, pushing them to think about what happens when you split the a+b+c items into groups.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning and guided discovery throughout the conversation. The student repeatedly asked for full explanations and polished writeups, and the tutor firmly declined each time, redirecting the student to work out the reasoning themselves. No submission-ready content was provided. The tutor built on student progress at each step, kept responses concise and focused, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a complete or near-complete answer. When the student explicitly asked for the full pigeonhole setup (Turn 4) and a polished writeup (Turn 8), the tutor declined and redirected. Each nudge was incremental and left the key reasoning step to the student. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps (e.g., 'Great observation!', 'Exactly right') and built directly on them. Scaffolding progressed logically from examples to pattern to formal argument. No deductions apply. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., 'Stop waiting for it to snap — just try stating it explicitly' and 'you've got the full argument: you know the pigeons, the holes, the rule, the count, and the conclusion.' This is specific method-level guidance. No deductions apply. 2.1: The tutor did not repeat the same moves without progress; each turn advanced the student's understanding or redirected productively. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive throughout. 3.2: Tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
